title: Trie (Prefix Tree)
description: A specialized tree for storing and searching strings efficiently -- each node has up to 26 children (one per letter), built with a HashMap instead of fixed left/right references.
objectives: Understand a Trie's structure: one node per character, shared prefixes merged
Implement startsWith, and understand a Trie's real-world use case: autocomplete

sections.text: Every tree so far (Binary Trees, BST) capped nodes at TWO children. A Trie (pronounced 'try', from re-TRIE-val) needs a variable number of children -- one per possible next character -- so instead of left/right fields, each node holds a Map from character to child node.
A tree specialized for storing strings, where each node represents one character and a path from the root spells out a prefix -- nodes are shared between words with common prefixes.
2. The Node: A HashMap of Children

children uses a HashMap (recall the Map lesson), mapping each possible next character to its child TrieNode. isEndOfWord marks whether a COMPLETE word ends at this exact node -- necessary because "cat" being stored doesn't mean "ca" is also a valid complete word, even though its nodes exist along the way.

sections.title: Tracing insert("cat") then insert("car") -- sharing the "ca" prefix
steps: insert("cat"): root -> c (new) -> a (new) -> t (new, isEndOfWord=true)
insert("car"): root -> c (EXISTS, reused) -> a (EXISTS, reused) -> r (new, isEndOfWord=true)
The nodes for 'c' and 'a' are SHARED between both words -- only 't' and 'r' branch separately
This sharing of common prefixes is the entire point of a Trie's name and structure

sections.text: putIfAbsent(c, new TrieNode()) only creates a new node if that character isn't ALREADY a child -- this is exactly what makes 'car' reuse the 'c' and 'a' nodes already created by 'cat', instead of duplicating them.

search("ca") returns false, even though the 'c' and 'a' nodes clearly exist (they're part of 'cat' and 'car') -- because isEndOfWord is false at that node. "ca" was never explicitly INSERTED as a complete word, only passed through on the way to longer words.

startsWith("ca") is true, unlike search("ca") which was false -- the difference is exactly isEndOfWord. startsWith only cares whether the PATH exists, not whether a complete word ends there. This is precisely how autocomplete suggestions work: typing 'ca' shows every word whose path passes through that point.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing search() (requires isEndOfWord true) with startsWith() (only requires the path to exist) -- 'ca' can be a valid prefix without being a valid complete word.
Forgetting to mark isEndOfWord = true at the end of insert() -- without it, no word would ever be found by search(), even if all its characters were inserted.
Using putIfAbsent incorrectly, or always creating a new node regardless of whether one exists -- this breaks prefix-sharing, defeating the Trie's whole purpose.

Assuming a Trie is limited to exactly 26 children like a fixed English-alphabet array -- using a Map (as done here) supports any character set naturally, at some cost to raw speed compared to a fixed-size array.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a Trie, and how does its structure differ from a binary tree?
questions.answer: A Trie is a tree specialized for storing strings, where each node represents one character and can have many children (one per possible next character), typically stored in a Map -- unlike a binary tree, which caps every node at exactly two children.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the isEndOfWord flag?
questions.answer: It marks whether a complete, explicitly inserted word ends at that exact node -- distinguishing a node that's merely part of a longer word's path from one that represents a full word by itself.

questions.question: What is the difference between search() and startsWith() in a Trie?
questions.answer: search() requires both that the full path exists AND that isEndOfWord is true at the final node -- it checks for a complete inserted word. startsWith() only requires the path to exist, regardless of isEndOfWord -- it checks for a valid prefix, which may or may not also be a complete word.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A Trie stores strings by giving each node a Map of children, one per possible next character, letting words with shared prefixes reuse the same nodes along their common path. isEndOfWord marks nodes where a complete inserted word actually ends, which is what distinguishes search() (requires a full path AND isEndOfWord true) from startsWith() (only requires the path to exist). This distinction is exactly what powers autocomplete: startsWith identifies every stored word sharing a typed prefix, without caring whether that prefix is itself a complete word.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Trie = a shared hallway of letters -- 'cat' and 'car' walk the SAME c-a hallway before splitting at the last letter. isEndOfWord = a door marked 'complete word here.' startsWith just checks the hallway exists; search also checks for that door.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: How does a Trie node's children differ from a binary tree node's left/right?
What does the isEndOfWord flag mark?
What's the key difference between search() and startsWith()?
What real-world feature is a Trie's startsWith() the foundation of?

sections.title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
sections.problemStatement: Implement a Trie with insert(word), search(word) (exact word match), and startsWith(prefix) (any word begins with prefix).

examples.input: insert("apple"); search("apple") -> true; search("app") -> false; startsWith("app") -> true; insert("app"); search("app") -> true

hints: Each node holds a fixed-size array (or map) of 26 children plus an isEndOfWord flag.
search() requires landing on a node with isEndOfWord true; startsWith() just requires reaching the last character's node.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Classic array-based Trie node with 26 children slots. insert walks/creates nodes per character and marks the last one as end-of-word. search/startsWith share a helper that walks the path and differ only in whether they also check the isEndOfWord flag.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(L) per operation, L = word length

sections.title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
sections.problemStatement: Design a data structure supporting addWord(word) and search(word), where search's word may contain '.' as a wildcard matching any single letter.

examples.input: addWord("bad"); addWord("dad"); addWord("mad"); search("pad") -> false; search("bad") -> true; search(".ad") -> true; search("b..") -> true

constraints: lowercase letters and '.' only in search queries
hints: search needs DFS/backtracking: on '.', try ALL 26 children recursively; on a normal letter, follow only that one child.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Trie insert identical to a standard trie. search uses a recursive helper that, upon hitting '.', tries every non-null child branch (backtracking), and upon a normal letter, follows just that one path.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(26^k) worst case for k dots, O(L) typical

sections.title: Replace Words
sections.problemStatement: Given a dictionary of root words and a sentence, replace every word in the sentence with the shortest root from the dictionary that is a prefix of it (if any).

examples.input: dictionary = ["cat","bat","rat"], sentence = "the cattle was rattled by the battery"
examples.output: "the cat was rat by the bat"

hints: Insert every root into a Trie.
For each sentence word, walk the Trie character by character; stop and replace as soon as you hit an isEndOfWord node (that's the SHORTEST matching root since you stop at the first match).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Insert all roots into a Trie. For each word, walk down the Trie following its characters; the moment an isEndOfWord node is reached, that prefix is the shortest valid root -- use it and stop. If no such node is reached, keep the original word.
approaches.output: "the cat was rat by the bat"

sections.title: Map Sum Pairs
sections.problemStatement: Design a structure with insert(key, val) (overwriting any previous value for that exact key) and sum(prefix) returning the sum of all values whose keys start with prefix.
hints: Keep a HashMap<String, Integer> of exact key -> value for overwrite handling.
For sum(prefix), the simplest correct approach is to iterate the map and add up values of matching keys -- a full Trie with subtree-sum caching is an optimization, not required for correctness.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store key -> value in a HashMap (so re-inserting the same key correctly overwrites, not adds). For sum(prefix), scan all entries and add up values whose key startsWith(prefix).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Trie where each node caches the sum of all values in its subtree. On insert, compute the delta versus any previous value for that exact key, then add that delta to every node along the insertion path. sum(prefix) becomes a direct O(prefix length) lookup.

sections.title: Word Search II
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n board of characters and a list of words, return all words that can be formed by a path of adjacent (up/down/left/right) cells, not reusing a cell within the same word.
hints: Backtracking each word individually against the board (like Word Search I) works but re-walks the board once per word -- wasteful when words share prefixes.
Better: insert all words into a Trie, then do ONE DFS pass over the board following Trie edges, so shared prefixes are explored together.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each word independently, run a full backtracking search (like LeetCode 79 Word Search) over the board. With many words this repeats the same board exploration over and over.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Insert all words into a Trie (storing the full word string at terminal nodes for easy retrieval). Do a single DFS from every board cell, following Trie children matching the board letters; when a Trie node marks a complete word, record it (and null out that word slot so it isn't added twice). Mark visited cells with a placeholder during the DFS and restore them on backtrack.
